decisionFrame.goal: Use the smallest vendor structure that still gives Abivax enough design challenge, controls discipline, and execution support to reach a January 1, 2027 finance-led go-live without turning Mike into the admin bottleneck.
decisionFrame.operatorBias: Keep one clear builder, one internal owner, and only a narrow external support layer. Avoid any second command structure that looks politically neat but creates duplicated governance.

knownFacts: Phase 1 should stay focused on core finance, reporting, and controls. O2C and Inventory are not required in the first wave.
The Oracle SOW is still broader than that target and includes P2P, R2R, O2C, and Design to Build / Inventory in one implementation scope.
CFGI sent a real two-phase implementation proposal on March 10, 2026: EUR 273K for phase 1 and EUR 390K total including phase 2.
KPMG's archived implementation-support proposal totals EUR 370.1K excluding VAT and expenses, but the source proposal is broader than the role Abivax should buy.
Internal finance bandwidth is a real constraint, so some external support remains justified even in a narrowed model.

decisionCriteria.label: Build accountability
decisionCriteria.prompt: Is there one firm that can be held directly accountable for configuration, integration, migration, and delivery?

decisionCriteria.label: Internal control
decisionCriteria.prompt: Does Mike remain the real day-to-day decision owner, or does the structure quietly move operational control outside the company?

decisionCriteria.label: Bandwidth relief
decisionCriteria.prompt: Does the support model remove admin-heavy burden from the finance team without creating extra reporting theater?

decisionCriteria.label: Design challenge
decisionCriteria.prompt: Who will push back if NetSuite over-scopes phase 1, weakens controls, or glosses over local execution issues?

decisionCriteria.label: Political clarity
decisionCriteria.prompt: Can Didier, Mike, NetSuite, and KPMG each explain their role in one sentence without overlap?

models.label: Model A
models.name: NetSuite implementation with no external overlay
models.summary: The leanest structure, but it leaves design challenge, controls pushback, and a large amount of coordination burden inside Abivax.
models.operatorRead: This is only sound if NetSuite proves a strong named team and Mike is willing to absorb more direct issue triage and testing coordination.
upsides: No ambiguity about who is building the system.
risks: NetSuite can dominate solution design unless Abivax pushes back effectively.
Mike becomes the de facto integration point for scope, controls, testing, and local coordination.
Finance bandwidth risk increases during UAT, cutover, and readiness.
models.bestWhen: Choose this only if NetSuite references are strong, the named delivery lead is senior, and Abivax can absorb testing/readiness admin internally.

models.label: Model B
models.name: NetSuite implements, KPMG supports selectively
models.summary: Keep NetSuite as the sole builder while using KPMG as a narrow challenge and support layer around controls, UAT, cutover, and France-side execution.
models.teamShape: NetSuite implementor + KPMG targeted support + Abivax core team
models.operatorRead: This is the strongest fit if KPMG is cut back hard. It preserves one builder, keeps Mike in charge, and still gives Abivax a brake system where NetSuite should be challenged.
upsides: Keeps build accountability with one implementor.
Reduces finance-team admin load during testing, readiness, and rollout.
Provides an independent controls and design challenge layer without turning KPMG into project owner.
risks: Fails quickly if KPMG is allowed to expand back into broad PMO governance.
Still requires a sharp RACI between Mike, NetSuite, and KPMG.
The named KPMG day-to-day lead matters more now that the role is narrow and execution-facing.
models.bestWhen: Best default if phase 1 is narrowed to finance/reporting/controls and KPMG scope is commercially reset around targeted support rather than PMO ownership.

models.label: Model C
models.name: CFGI as implementation lead
models.summary: Potentially cleaner than a three-firm model if CFGI can truly own delivery, but the evidence is not yet strong enough to switch during board week.
models.teamShape: CFGI implementation lead + Abivax core team
models.operatorRead: This remains a plausible fallback path, not the current recommendation. It needs named-team diligence, reference calls, and a clearer answer on French execution support.
upsides: Could solve the concern about NetSuite-led implementation quality directly.
Cleaner role structure than a three-firm setup if CFGI can own delivery end to end.
Their two-phase view supports the narrower phase 1 boundary.
risks: Insufficient diligence today on named leadership, French stakeholder fit, and operating cadence.
A switch now would destabilize the board narrative.
Still leaves questions around controls support and on-the-ground coordination.
models.bestWhen: Revisit only after references, staffing diligence, and a sharper implementation RACI prove CFGI is meaningfully safer than NetSuite.

models.label: Model D
models.name: CFGI implementation with KPMG targeted support
models.summary: Structurally possible, but not a good near-term move because it introduces a builder switch and an overlay at the same time.
models.teamShape: CFGI implementation lead + KPMG targeted support + Abivax core team
models.operatorRead: This is a contingency model, not an execution recommendation for this week. It creates too many moving parts unless both firms have unusually crisp swimlanes.
upsides: Independent design challenge can still exist if CFGI becomes builder.
Potentially stronger controls and cutover support than CFGI alone.
risks: Easy to create duplicated meetings and issue ownership confusion.
Mike can get trapped between builder language and support language again.
Hard to justify before a board decision without more diligence.
models.bestWhen: Only consider after a deliberate post-board redesign, not as a board-week change.

minimumViableModel.summary: The minimum viable model is Didier as sponsor, Mike as internal ERP Program Director, NetSuite as sole implementor, a small finance-business owner group for decisions/signoff, one IT/integration owner, and a narrowed KPMG lane for design challenge, UAT, cutover, and selective France-side coordination.
requiredRoles: Didier as executive sponsor and final escalation point
Mike as internal ERP Program Director and day-to-day decision owner
NetSuite as the only builder accountable for system delivery
Finance business owners for requirements, design choices, and signoff
IT/integration owner for interfaces, access, and technical dependencies
KPMG only for targeted support where internal bandwidth is weakest
minimumViableModel.whyLeanEnough: It removes the second command structure while preserving the few external support lanes that reduce operational risk for a bandwidth-constrained finance team.
whereItBreaks: If NetSuite staffing is weak and no one challenges solution design.
If KPMG is cut so far back that finance is left to run UAT admin and cutover coordination alone.
If Hema and Mike roles are blurred and decisions do not have one clear internal owner.

roleTitleRecommendation.why: It signals real internal ownership of scope, vendors, decisions, and escalation without sounding like vendor PMO or generic change management.
roleTitleRecommendation.useInApp: Use ERP Program Director as the operating-role label on ERP pages and governance materials.
roleTitleRecommendation.useExternal: Use Mike Markman, ERP Program Director, for board and vendor-facing ERP materials.
roleTitleRecommendation.keepHrTitle: Keep VP, Global ERP Lead in identity/profile data where the point is Mike's actual corporate title.

antiPatterns: Do not let KPMG become a second command structure with broad PMO ownership, weekly governance theater, and duplicate escalation paths.
Do not treat NetSuite platform selection as proof that NetSuite should define all functional scope in phase 1.
Do not switch to CFGI during board week based on a credible proposal alone; that is still too speculative without deeper diligence.
Do not remove all external support if the practical result is that finance has to run documentation, testing admin, and cutover tracking on top of day jobs.
internalRedFlags: The current delivery-model page still encoded KPMG PMO as the recommended model before this refresh.
Several presentation artifacts still use PMO language that is broader than the desired role structure.
The app still lacks a canonical decision ledger and diligence tracker, so some role and vendor assumptions are embedded in page copy instead of reusable data.
evidenceStillNeeded: Named NetSuite delivery lead, backup lead, and France / US staffing model for phase 1.
Reference-call confirmation on how NetSuite handled scope discipline, data migration, UAT, cutover, and executive escalation.
Commercially narrowed KPMG statement of work aligned to targeted support only.
Final phase 1 scope lock for reporting, controls, P2P boundaries, and deferred items.
Explicit RACI showing Didier sponsors, Mike runs, NetSuite builds, and KPMG supports selectively.
